agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"CEO described a dynamic pricing product called DynaPricing at an all-hands meeting. The description is high-level, aspirational, and lacks specificity on thresholds, boundaries, metrics, and operational details. This is a vision statement, not a specification.","bounded_context":"Pricing / Rate Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"Undefined measure of delivery request volume or urgency in a given area/timeframe — exact metric not specified"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds not defined"},{"term":"Real time","definition":"Pricing adjustments that respond to current conditions — latency tolerance not specified (seconds? minutes? per-order?)"},{"term":"Delivery price","definition":"The fee charged to the customer for a courier delivery — base price structure not defined"},{"term":"Competitive","definition":"Pricing positioned relative to market alternatives — no benchmark or competitor set defined"},{"term":"Profitable","definition":"Revenue from delivery fees exceeds cost of fulfillment — margin targets not specified"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business pricing policy. The direction and magnitude of adjustment is a business decision that could be changed or overridden.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN demand exceeds the high-demand threshold WHEN a delivery price is calculated THEN the price must be higher than the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business pricing policy. Management could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN demand falls below the low-demand threshold WHEN a delivery price is calculated THEN the price must be lower than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual intervention required per transaction","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a design choice. Business could decide to add manual approval for extreme cases.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN any demand signal change WHEN the system recalculates prices THEN no human approval step is required before the new price takes effect"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Prices must be bounded by guardrails to prevent extreme values","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Unbounded pricing could produce zero-dollar or infinite prices, corrupting financial state and potentially violating consumer protection law. Some boundary must always exist.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN any demand level WHEN a price is calculated THEN the resulting price must fall within the configured minimum and maximum price bounds"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A delivery cannot have a zero or negative price — this is a fundamental financial integrity constraint. A zero price means free delivery with no cost recovery; a negative price means paying the customer to deliver.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN any demand condition WHEN a price is calculated THEN the price is strictly greater than zero"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Price adjustments must be based on current demand signals, not stale data","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The definition of 'current' is a policy choice (seconds vs minutes). But the intent that pricing reflects present conditions is a business policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a demand signal older than the configured staleness threshold WHEN a price is requested THEN the system must either refresh the signal or fall back to a safe default price"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"Demand in a delivery zone exceeds the high-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price quote","then":"The quoted price is higher than the base price for that zone"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"Demand in a delivery zone is below the low-demand threshold","when":"A customer requests a delivery price quote","then":"The quoted price is lower than the base price for that zone"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Any demand condition, including extreme spikes","when":"A price is calculated","then":"The price is clamped between the configured price floor and price ceiling"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A demand signal changes","when":"The system recalculates pricing","then":"No manual approval or human intervention is required"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A calculated price after demand adjustment","when":"The price is finalized","then":"The price is strictly greater than zero"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand is exactly at the boundary between 'high' and 'normal'","expected_behavior":"System must have a deterministic rule for boundary values — no oscillation or price flickering"},{"scenario":"Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 100x normal)","expected_behavior":"Price ceiling guardrail activates; price cannot exceed configured maximum regardless of demand multiplier"},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero (no requests in a zone)","expected_behavior":"Price floor guardrail activates; price cannot go below configured minimum. System must not produce a negative or zero price."},{"scenario":"Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale","expected_behavior":"System must fall back to a safe default price rather than serving a price based on outdated or missing data"},{"scenario":"Two near-simultaneous requests in a zone where demand is crossing a threshold","expected_behavior":"Both customers should receive consistent pricing — no race condition where one gets surge and the other doesn't for the same conditions"},{"scenario":"Price adjustment would make delivery unprofitable (below cost of fulfillment)","expected_behavior":"Unclear from spec — profitability constraint implies a cost-based floor, but no cost data is defined"},{"scenario":"Competitor pricing is significantly lower during a surge","expected_behavior":"Unclear from spec — 'competitive' goal conflicts with 'profitable' goal during high demand. No resolution strategy defined."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, then price changes before order confirmation","expected_behavior":"Unclear — no price lock / quote validity window defined"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue per delivery. Upside: higher margins during peak demand. Downside: poorly calibrated guardrails could either leave money on the table (ceiling too low) or drive customers to competitors (ceiling too high). A missing price floor could result in below-cost deliveries.","operational":"Fully automated pricing removes manual rate management overhead but introduces operational risk: monitoring, alerting, and fallback mechanisms are critical. Pricing errors at scale propagate instantly with no human buffer.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge pricing without explanation causes dissatisfaction and churn. Low-demand discounts could drive positive engagement if surfaced proactively.","risk":"High risk due to spec vagueness. No defined thresholds, no guardrail values, no fallback strategy, no price lock mechanism, no audit trail requirements mentioned. Consumer protection regulations around dynamic/surge pricing are not addressed. Reputational risk if perceived as price gouging during emergencies.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will pay variable prices; trust and satisfaction depend on transparency, fairness perception, and magnitude of swings"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Higher demand pricing may attract more couriers to high-demand zones — or courier pay model interaction is undefined"},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Need to define profitability thresholds, margin targets, and cost-of-delivery baselines that feed into guardrails"},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Must provide reliable demand signals; pricing depends on demand data quality and freshness"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Dynamic pricing has regulatory implications in many jurisdictions — price gouging laws, consumer protection, transparency requirements"},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"Market response to DynaPricing could trigger a pricing war or competitive repositioning"}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement with almost no specifics. Every meaningful implementation detail is undefined: demand metrics, thresholds, guardrail values, base price structure, real-time latency requirements, competitive benchmarks, profitability targets, and regulatory constraints. The rules extracted are directionally correct but untestable without concrete parameters.","high_confidence_areas":["Price goes up when demand is high (directionally stated)","Price goes down when demand is low (directionally stated)","System is automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though vaguely)"],"low_confidence_areas":["What metric defines 'demand' (order volume? requests per minute? zone saturation?)","What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand thresholds","What the guardrail bounds are (price floor and ceiling values)","What 'real time' means in terms of latency","How 'competitive' and 'profitable' are balanced when they conflict","Whether a base price exists and how it is determined","Courier compensation interaction with dynamic pricing","Regulatory and legal compliance requirements","Price quote validity window and locking semantics","Audit and transparency requirements"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are made up or down","'Guardrails' means at minimum a price floor and ceiling","Demand is measured per delivery zone or geographic area","The system must produce a positive price under all conditions","Some form of demand signal infrastructure already exists or will be built"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is undefined — it could mean order volume per zone, requests per time window, courier utilization rate, or other metrics. The pricing model cannot be built without a precise demand definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Finance"],"options":["Define demand as order request count per zone per time window","Define demand as courier utilization ratio (requests / available couriers)","Define demand as a composite index of multiple signals"],"recommendation":"Define demand as courier utilization ratio (requests / available couriers) as it directly reflects supply-demand imbalance, which is the core business driver."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails so prices don't go crazy' — no thresholds, bounds, or escalation rules defined. 'Probably' suggests this isn't even confirmed as a requirement.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Finance"],"options":["Hard price floor and ceiling as absolute bounds","Percentage-based max deviation from base price","Tiered guardrails with different bounds by zone or time of day"],"recommendation":"Implement hard price floor (at or above cost of delivery) and ceiling (e.g., 3x base price) as non-negotiable guardrails, with configurable multipliers per zone."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive but also profitable' presents a tension with no resolution strategy. During high demand, maximizing profit means raising prices, which may not be competitive. During low demand, being competitive means lowering prices, which may not be profitable.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product","Customers"],"options":["Profitability always wins — never price below cost","Competitiveness always wins — match market within margin tolerance","Configurable priority per zone or business phase"],"recommendation":"Establish a hard profitability floor (never below cost of delivery) and allow competitive adjustments only above that floor. Escalate to product leadership for explicit priority ranking."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined — could mean per-request recalculation, minute-by-minute batch updates, or periodic refresh. This fundamentally affects architecture, cost, and customer experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Customers"],"options":["Per-request: recalculate on every price quote","Periodic: recalculate every N minutes and cache","Event-driven: recalculate when demand signal crosses a threshold"],"recommendation":"Event-driven with a minimum recalculation interval (e.g., 60 seconds) to balance responsiveness with system stability."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Fully automated' does not clarify whether there is any human override capability, kill switch, or approval process for extreme scenarios (e.g., natural disasters, system errors).","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Legal/Compliance","Customers"],"options":["Fully automated with no override","Automated with manual kill switch for emergencies","Automated with approval gates above certain thresholds"],"recommendation":"Automated with a manual kill switch and configurable alert thresholds — 'fully automated' for normal operations but with emergency human override capability."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

This is a vision statement, not a specification.","bounded_context":"Pricing / Rate Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system"},{"term":"Demand","defini

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this CEO description of "DynaPricing" and extract domain rules.

This is a vague, high-level description from a non-technical stakeholder. Key observations:
- "adjust delivery prices in real time based on demand" - dynamic pricing
- "demand high → prices up, demand low → prices down" - basic supply/demand
- "competitive but also profitable" - tension between two goals, no specifics
- "fully automated" - no manual intervention
- "probably have some guardrails" - vague, "probably"
- "prices don't go crazy" - undefined bounds


- Everything here is ambiguous—no specific thresholds, no defined guardrails, unclear what "real time," "demand," or "competitive" actually mean, so I need to flag this for clarification with confidence at or below 55.
